Transcrição Completa: 56 How To Keep Your Team Accountable

 

[00:00] How to keep your team accountable without micromanaging really comes down to the dichotomy of your leadership.

 

[00:07] There's a great book written by Jocko can't I don't remember his last name. The guy who wrote Extreme Ownership long story short has a book called The Dichotomy of Leadership.

 

[00:18] Inside of one of my clients business operations Dan Lok. He has an incredible leader. His name's Desmond.

 

[00:26] And what Desmond does inside of Dan's business is truly empower the other staff members to step up in their individual roles to become more of a leader.

 

[00:36] And as Dan as an individual continues to expand as a business mogul, Desmond continues to expand as a business mogul.

 

[00:44] And every team player that they have, they have over 22 different staff become empowered as leaders.

 

[00:52] And as the team continues scaling, they find every two to three months or so, they bring in new staff.

 

[00:58] And the people that they empowered to become leaders are now managing other people that will eventually become leaders as well.

 

[01:05] The point is in a successful business operation like somebody as an example as Dan Lok who's doing two to $2.5 million per month and continuing to scale aggressively.

 

[01:17] That is an incredible example of an initial leader empowering somebody else to become a leader and then empowering others to become a leader.

 

[01:25] So as they continue scaling, they have this giant leader pyramid.

 

[01:29] So my concept here that I'm giving you is inside your staff, you start off as a leader yourself.

 

[01:35] Even if it's just you right now, eventually the first person that you bring into your agency or the first person that you start working with, you're going to start duplicating your values and your skills and how you yourself are accountable to get things done.

 

[01:50] And that's likely how the other person is going to be holding themselves to get things done.

 

[01:54] Accountability, just like social learning, is apparent and something that's already going on that's observed that gets inherently integrated into this new staff member's day-to-day.

 

[02:06] So, for example, if you throughout your day are taking notes on yourself and self-reflecting, you have a higher probability that the staff around you are also going to be participating in that skill or at least starting off with asking questions about why it's being done.

 

[02:23] If you're explaining the true benefit in something that would potentially impact this person's work, make them more productive, make them more efficient, or just get them better results in life, they have a high probability of doing it, too.

 

[02:36] However, if you bring something to somebody that you want them to do, such as take notes on themselves to increase their self-awareness throughout the day, they have a much lower probability of doing it.

 

[02:47] If you tell them to do it, and then several times throughout the day, you come in and check and see if they're doing it, and even sit next to them while they're doing it.

 

[02:56] That's an example of micromanaging comparatively.

 

[02:59] If you want your staff to start getting better results, communicating with clients more frequently, communicating with clients more effectively as well, you yourself lead the process of how that's done, how it works, and what it takes to do what you want to do.

 

[03:15] Duplicate yourself into your people. Breed accountability by leading them, not micromanaging them.

 

[03:22] Have a culture in your agency of accountability, of a result driven focus at all times.

 

[03:28] But yourself and the leaders that you integrate into your team all have to have those inherent traits and values constantly being projected onto the staff by doing it yourself and having the other people a part of your team take note of that through social learning.

 

[03:44] They'll adapt and then all of a sudden you'll have this culture of accountability and constant focus on results.

 

[03:51] Of course, there's notifications that you can use in different project management tools. clickup.com, one of my favorite tools that you can use for project management, gives you the ability when you're integrating to add notifications that come to people.

 

[04:07] Trello is another example. There's all kinds of different softwares out there that you can use that will give notifications to people for when they need to do things.

 

[04:16] However, most of those are ineffective and people you likely will ignore them unless there's already people that are using it and the person who has the opportunity to use it can see the advantages of doing it.

 

[04:29] That's where true accountability becomes integrated into your business at higher levels than it will if you just simply tell people, "Hey, make sure you're doing this, make sure you're doing that, make sure you're doing this, make sure you" you're constantly checking in on them.

 

[04:43] That doesn't create inspiration. It doesn't create accountability. That'll create a demotivating culture with a high churn rate of employees, I imagine.

 

[04:52] Okay. Lead and breed accountability by leadership, not by micromanaging. I'll see you in the next video.

 

 

📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO

 

NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:

Dan Lok: $2M a $2.5M por mês em receita

22+ membros de staff (todos empoderados como líderes)

Contratação de novos staff a cada 2-3 meses

Crescimento agressivo e contínuo

 

ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:

 

Modelo de Liderança Piramidal:

Líder inicial (Dan Lok) → Líder principal (Desmond) → 22+ líderes de equipe

Cada líder empoderado cria novos líderes

Crescimento exponencial de liderança

 

Princípio de Duplicação:

Duplicar valores pessoais na equipe

Duplicar habilidades e competências

Duplicar accountability (responsabilização)

Liderar pelo exemplo, não por ordem

 

Social Learning (Aprendizado Social):

Staff observa comportamentos do líder

Integração natural de hábitos e práticas

Perguntas surgem organicamente sobre processos observados

Adaptação por inspiração, não imposição

 

Exemplos Práticos de Liderança:

Tomar notas sobre si mesmo e auto-reflexão

Comunicação frequente e efetiva com clientes

Demonstrar benefícios reais das práticas

Explicar impacto em produtividade e eficiência

 

MICROMANAGING vs LIDERANÇA:

 

Micromanaging (Evitar):

Dizer o que fazer repetidamente

Verificar múltiplas vezes ao dia

Sentar ao lado para supervisionar

Resultado: cultura desmotivadora, alta rotatividade

 

Liderança Efetiva (Aplicar):

Liderar pelo exemplo

Demonstrar processos pessoalmente

Criar cultura de accountability

Foco constante em resultados

Projetar valores através de ações

 

FERRAMENTAS DE GESTÃO:

ClickUp.com (ferramenta favorita mencionada)

Trello

Sistemas de notificação em ferramentas de project management

IMPORTANTE: Ferramentas são ineficazes sem cultura de accountability estabelecida

 

ESTRUTURA:

 

Framework de Accountability:

Líder demonstra comportamento desejado

Staff observa através de social learning

Staff questiona e entende benefícios

Staff adapta naturalmente

Cultura de accountability se estabelece

Novos membros absorvem cultura existente

 

Conceito Chave - Dichotomy of Leadership:

Referência ao livro de Jocko Willink

Baseado em "Extreme Ownership"

Balance entre autonomia e direção

Empoderamento sem micromanaging

 

Pirâmide de Liderança:

Topo: Fundador/CEO

Segundo nível: Líderes principais

Terceiro nível: Líderes de equipe (22+)

Base em expansão: Novos staff se tornando líderes

 

DADOS FINANCEIROS:

Caso Dan Lok: $2M - $2.5M/mês

Crescimento agressivo contínuo

Escalabilidade através de liderança multiplicada

ROI em desenvolvimento de líderes (não especificado numericamente)

 

PRINCÍPIOS FUNDAMENTAIS:

"Duplicate yourself into your people"

"Breed accountability by leading them, not micromanaging them"

"Lead and breed accountability by leadership, not by micromanaging"

Cultura > Ferramentas

Inspiração > Imposição

Exemplo > Instrução

 

DURAÇÃO: 04:59 FORMATO: Vídeo educacional direto para câmera, estilo monólogo instrucional
